Writing & Voice

Language as
design material,
not decoration

Every word in an interface is a decision. Navigation labels, experience descriptions, error messages, onboarding copy — each one either reduces cognitive load or adds to it. Writing isn't what happens after design. It shapes what design becomes.

06
Writing Types
03
Research Personas
08
Documents Produced
01
Voice, Consistently Applied
01 — From Research to Understanding

Research produces data.
Personas produce people.

Interviews, observation, and synthesis surface patterns. But patterns don't make design decisions — people do. Personas translate research findings into human stories: specific individuals with specific goals, frustrations, and ways of moving through the world. Once you can picture who you're designing for, every decision has a reason.

For Botanic Elixir, three personas were developed from audience research into London's creative and professional community. Each persona was written before any layout decisions were made — because the people had to exist before the space could be designed for them.

Saoirse
26 · Freelance Copywriter · East London
Experience: Writing Sessions
Goals
  • A space outside her flat that doesn't feel like a co-working office
  • Consistent ambient noise — enough to focus, not enough to distract
  • Copy that writes itself when the environment is right
Frustrations
  • Coffee shops that play music too loud or change the playlist mid-session
  • Being asked to move when she's been there for two hours
  • Spaces that perform creativity without actually supporting it
"I don't need inspiration. I need a table, good light, and somewhere that doesn't make me feel like I should leave."
What this shaped

Writing Sessions copy was written for Saoirse first. The language is permission-giving rather than energising — it acknowledges that she already knows what she needs, and the space is ready when she is.

Marcus
22 · UCL Computer Science Student · Bloomsbury
Experience: Game Night
Goals
  • An evening that's genuinely different from the pub or someone's flat
  • Something to do as a group that doesn't require a lot of planning
  • A space that makes his group of friends feel like they chose well
Frustrations
  • Venues that are either too loud to talk or too quiet to feel social
  • Booking systems that require commitment three weeks in advance
  • Experiences that feel designed for couples or solo visitors, not groups
"We just want somewhere that feels like an event without having to make it one ourselves."
What this shaped

Game Night copy is the most socially-pitched of the six experiences. It acknowledges the group dynamic — "come in a group, leave with a reason to come back" — and treats the decision to visit as already validated.

Priya
29 · Product Manager & Yoga Instructor · Islington
Experience: Wellness
Goals
  • A space that takes the word "wellness" seriously rather than aesthetically
  • Community — people who share her values without needing her to perform them
  • Something between a café and a retreat that fits into a weekday
Frustrations
  • Wellness spaces that are expensive, exclusive, or both
  • Cafés that claim to support focus but have nowhere comfortable to sit for an hour
  • Language that overclaims — "transformative", "sacred space", "ritual" used without meaning
"I want somewhere that actually supports how I want to feel. Not somewhere that just says it does."
What this shaped

Wellness copy avoids every word Priya's frustration flagged. No "sacred". No "transformative". Instead: "A space that takes the word seriously." That's Priya's language turned back into copy — which is exactly how persona-driven writing is supposed to work.

These three people don't exist. But every decision made for Botanic Elixir — the navigation structure, the copy register, the spatial logic of each experience — was made as if they did. That's what personas are for: not to describe a market segment, but to make the people in it real enough to design for.

02 — Writing in Projects

Where the writing shows up

Writing isn't content dropped into finished design — it's the material decisions are made from. Navigation labels determine what pages need to exist. Experience descriptions determine how they're structured. Error messages determine what states need to be designed for.

"Language is the first design decision. The words came first — the structure followed."
01
UX Writing
Navigation labels, microcopy, error states, onboarding sequences — copy that reduces cognitive load rather than adding to it.
02
Academic Writing
Research documentation, analysis, theoretical framing — structured argumentation that connects evidence to conclusions.
03
Requirements
Functional and non-functional specifications — writing that makes constraints explicit and traceable to decisions.
04
Reflective
Process documentation and critical reflection — writing that examines why decisions were made after they've been made.
01
UX Writing
Botanic Elixir
Navigation Copy & Experience Language
UX Copy Microcopy Voice & Tone
Live Site ↗
Copy written before layout

For Botanic Elixir, writing came first. Navigation labels, experience descriptions, and mood-based copy were drafted before wireframes existed — because the language determined what the structure needed to be, not the other way around. Each of the six experiences required its own tone: Focus & Create is quiet and purposeful, Game Night is social and slightly irreverent, Restorative Retreat is slow and permission-giving.

Six experience voices
Focus & Create

A quiet corner with strong light and stronger coffee. For the mornings when the work needs to actually happen.

Writing Sessions

Words need room. Comfortable seating, low noise, and the kind of atmosphere that makes the page feel less blank.

Game Night

Come in a group. Leave with a reason to come back. We make space for the people who turn an evening into a ritual.

Restorative Retreat

Some visits aren't about productivity. Slow down. Stay longer than you planned. That's the point.

Wellness

A space that takes the word seriously. Botanically-led drinks, quieter zones, and an environment built for recovery.

Date Night

Something between ambiance and intention. For evenings that deserve more than a restaurant and a bill.

Research Foundation — User Voice

The language decisions in the Apple Watch project did not come from the brief. They came from Janet, James, and Emily — three participants from the Apple Watch user study whose voices, goals, and frustrations were synthesized before a single line of copy was written.

02
Academic Writing
Hypertext & Hypermedia
Origins, Evolution & Contemporary Applications
Research Paper Argumentation Citation
Structure as argument

A formal research paper examining the historical development of hypertext from Douglas Engelbart's oN-Line System through Tim Berners-Lee's World Wide Web to contemporary applications in software engineering, narrative environments, and media distribution. The paper argues that hypertext and hypermedia are not tools layered onto the web — they are the underlying design principles that determine how digital information is organised, accessed, and understood.

Key argument

Rather than structuring information as a fixed, linear sequence, hypertext organises content through linked relationships — meaning is generated through connections between ideas, not through a predetermined reading order. This same principle governs how interfaces should be structured: users navigate by relationship and association, not by following a path someone else designed for them.

03
Requirements Writing
Apple Watch Health System
Functional, Experiential & Accessibility Requirements
Requirements User Needs Accessibility
Writing from the user's perspective

System requirements written from research data rather than feature lists. Each requirement was categorised — Functional, Experiential, Informational, Accessibility — and written in plain language from the user's point of view rather than the system's capabilities. "The system must allow users to monitor key health metrics in one consolidated view so that they can easily understand their overall wellness" is a user need, not a technical specification.

What this writing taught

Requirements writing revealed that the difference between a good requirement and a bad one is almost always a question of whose perspective it's written from. Feature-first language describes what a system does. Need-first language describes what a person is trying to accomplish. The discipline of writing requirements correctly is the discipline of keeping users at the centre — the same discipline that drives every other writing type in this portfolio.

04
Descriptive Writing
Video Transcription
Visual & Auditory Description — Accessibility Writing
Transcription Descriptive Writing Accessibility
Making the visual verbal

A second-by-second transcription of a YouTube video — describing both visual elements and auditory content with enough precision that someone who couldn't see the screen could fully understand what was happening. This required translating spatial relationships, movement, expression, and implied emotion into written language. "His arms and knees are bent, with his cylindrical hands on his head. His eyes are wide, his eyebrows slope downwards, and the mouth is agape" — that level of specificity is the difference between description and accessibility.

Connection to UX writing

Descriptive writing and UX writing share the same core challenge: translating what is visually or spatially obvious into language that works for someone who cannot rely on that visual context. Writing alt text, ARIA labels, and interface descriptions requires the same discipline as video transcription — precision, economy, and the user's perspective over the designer's.

05
Reflective Writing
Research & Process Reflection
Interview Analysis, AI Usage & Design Evolution
Reflection Analysis Process Documentation
Writing that examines process

Three reflective documents — an interview bias analysis, an AI tool usage reflection, and a design evolution paper — each use writing as a tool for examining practice rather than reporting outcomes. The interview analysis identifies specific moments where bias appeared and explains what the interviewer should have done instead. The AI usage reflection distinguishes between tools that assisted process and tools that could not substitute for original thinking. The design evolution paper traces the conceptual thread connecting DOCR, Botanic Elixir, and environmental psychology.

Why reflective writing matters in design

Designers who cannot articulate why they made a decision are designers who cannot improve. Reflective writing builds the habit of examining process — not just outcomes — and that habit is what separates iterative practice from repetition. Every reflection in this portfolio made the next project better.

03 — The Practice

Writing types, illustrated

Each type of writing below is paired with the actual document it produced — showing not just what was written, but why that form of writing existed and what it contributed to the larger work.

✍
UX Copy
Navigation, microcopy, experience descriptions — language designed to orient, not decorate
📚
Academic
Research writing, theoretical framing, structured argumentation with traceable evidence
☷
Requirements
Functional specifications — writing that makes constraints explicit and decisions non-arbitrary
📌
Descriptive
Transcript and observational documentation — faithful record without interpretation injected
☌
Reflective
Critical process documentation — examining what was decided and why, after the fact
01
UX Writing & Copy
Words that reduce friction, orient users, and carry a brand voice without calling attention to themselves. Good UX writing disappears into the interface — the user never notices it because it does exactly what they needed.
Live Site ↗
Design Evolution — From Research to Interface to Environment
1 / 
UX Copy & Voice — Connection Document
Language that connects research to design

This document traces the conceptual thread that runs from environmental psychology research through the DOCR redesign through to Botanic Elixir. The writing had to hold together ideas across three different domains — academic research, interface design, and spatial experience — while remaining accessible and clear.

Writing was the tool that made the connection visible — without language, the relationship between physical and digital space remains intuitive but inarticulable
The document moves from observation to analysis to implication — the structure of the writing mirrors the structure of the research process itself
Every sentence earns its place by advancing the argument rather than restating it
Connected to the work

The discipline of writing this document — finding language precise enough to connect environmental psychology to interface design — directly shaped how Botanic Elixir's experience descriptions were written. Both required the same precision: words that carry meaning without adding noise.

02
Academic & Research Writing
Structured argument supported by evidence, written for a reader who will question every claim. Academic writing is not about demonstrating knowledge — it is about constructing a position that can withstand scrutiny and contributing something to an existing conversation.
Origins, Evolution & Contemporary Applications of Hypermedia and Hypertext
1 / 
Research Paper — Hypertext & Hypermedia
Building an argument across 10 pages

A formally structured research paper examining the historical development of hypertext from its earliest conceptualisation through to contemporary applications. The paper argues that hypertext is not a feature of the web but a fundamental design principle — and that understanding this principle changes how we think about information architecture, interface design, and user navigation.

Organised around a central thesis rather than a topic survey — every section advances the argument rather than adding information for its own sake
Academic writing forces clarity: if a claim cannot be supported, it cannot appear
The discipline of citation — representing sources accurately — is the same discipline required when writing about users: their words and experiences must be represented honestly, not shaped to fit a preferred conclusion
Connected to the work

The argument this paper makes — that navigating information is shaped by relationships between ideas, not by predetermined paths — became the theoretical foundation for Botanic Elixir's mood-based navigation. A user chooses their experience based on their intent and emotional state, not based on a product hierarchy someone else imposed.

03
Requirements Writing
Translating research findings into actionable, testable statements of what a system must do — from the user's perspective, not the engineer's. Requirements writing is where research becomes specification, and specification becomes product.
Apple Watch Health System — Requirements & Categorisation
1 / 
System Requirements — Apple Watch Health
Four requirements, four categories, one user

System requirements developed from Apple Watch user research, categorised into Functional, Experiential, Informational, and Accessibility-related needs. Each requirement is written from the user's perspective, specifying what the user must be able to do and why — not what the system must technically implement.

Requirement 1 (Functional): consolidated health metrics view so users understand their overall wellness without switching between apps
Requirement 4 (Accessibility): adjustable display, contrast, and notification feedback so users with different sensory needs can use the device comfortably
The categorisation process itself is a form of synthesis — grouping requirements by type reveals patterns in what users actually need versus what features a product happens to have
Connected to the work

Writing requirements correctly — in plain language, from the user's perspective — is the same discipline as writing good UX copy. Both ask: what does this person need to understand, and what language will help them understand it without adding friction?

04
Descriptive & Transcription Writing
Translating the visual and auditory into language — with enough precision that someone who cannot see or hear the source material can fully understand it. This is the foundation of accessible writing, and it is harder than it looks.
Video Transcription — Pattern Weaver Mind
1 / 
Descriptive Transcription — Video Accessibility
Second by second, visual and auditory

A complete transcription of a YouTube video documenting both visual elements (animation, spatial relationships, character expression, movement) and auditory content (narration, tone, music) at 5-second intervals. The goal: someone who cannot see the video should be able to understand everything happening in it — not just what is being said, but what is being shown and why it matters to the communication.

Visual description required translating implied emotion into explicit language — "his eyebrows slope downwards and the mouth is agape" rather than "he looks panicked"
Spatial relationships between elements had to be described relative to each other — not just "there is a graduation cap" but "on the bottom right of the person, near the person, is a graduation cap floating, slightly crooked"
Auditory description included both content and tone — the piano's presence was noted because it shaped the emotional register of the narration
Connected to the work

The precision required for transcription is the same precision required for alt text, ARIA labels, and screen reader navigation. Every accessibility writing decision in Botanic Elixir's build was informed by this exercise in describing what is visually obvious to someone who cannot see it.

05
Reflective Writing
Writing that examines process, not just outcome. Reflective writing is how practice improves — by articulating what happened, what should have happened differently, and what that means for the next decision.
Interview Analysis — Bias, Probing & Reflection
1 / 
Reflective Writing — Interview Process Analysis
What the transcript revealed that the interview didn't

An analysis of a peer interview on academic deadline management, written after reviewing the transcript. The reflection identifies specific moments where bias appeared — leading questions, affirmative backchanneling, missed probing opportunities — and explains what each one cost the research.

Identifies confirmation bias by name and traces it to specific exchanges — not a general acknowledgment of imperfection but a precise account of what happened and why
Names a specific missed probing moment — the participant mentioning lost sleep — and explains what questions should have followed and what data was lost by not asking them
Ends with a genuine surprise — the participant's system was more sophisticated than expected — which models the right relationship between a researcher and their assumptions
Connected to the work

Reflective writing is the discipline that turns single experiences into transferable lessons. The bias identified in this interview analysis didn't just improve future interviews — it changed how Botanic Elixir's persona research was framed, how the competitive analysis was conducted, and how every piece of writing in this portfolio was revised.

Writing Support Reflection & Research Process Guide
1 / 
Process Reflection — AI Tools & Academic Writing
Tools that assist versus tools that replace

A documented reflection on the use of Grammarly, ChatGPT, and Microsoft Copilot during the development of the hypertext research paper. The document distinguishes clearly between what each tool contributed — grammar and punctuation review, brainstorming and structural organisation, wording and readability suggestions — and what remained entirely the writer's responsibility: academic decisions, source evaluation, interpretation, and final content.

Grammarly assisted with sentence-level clarity — a mechanical aid that does not affect thinking
ChatGPT supported brainstorming and structural organisation — useful for seeing options, not for making decisions
The reflection also documents 16 questions used to evaluate sources — a research process guide that demonstrates how rigorous source evaluation works
Connected to the work

The same question this document asks about AI tools — does this assist my thinking or substitute for it? — applies to every tool in a design process. Templates, component libraries, AI-generated copy: each one is useful until it starts making decisions that belong to the designer. Knowing the difference is the skill.

04 — Why It Matters

Writing doesn't support design —
it is design

"Every word in an interface is a decision. Each one either reduces cognitive load — or adds to it."
Before Layout
Copy written first changes what gets builtFor Botanic Elixir, the six experience descriptions existed before any wireframe. "Focus & Create" — quiet corner, strong light — determined the spatial logic of the Experiences page. When copy comes first, design solves a real problem. When design comes first, language becomes decoration.
Navigation
Labels are architecture decisions, not naming exercises"How It Works" tells a first-time visitor exactly what to expect. "About Our Space" does not. The choice of label determines whether a page gets clicked — and whether the page it links to can deliver on what was promised.
AI & Tools
Tools that assist versus tools that replaceThe same question the AI usage reflection asks applies to every design tool: does this assist my thinking, or substitute for it? Templates, component libraries, AI-generated copy — each is useful until it starts making decisions that belong to the designer.
01
UX copy written before layout changes what gets built

For Botanic Elixir, writing the six experience descriptions before designing any screens forced the structure to serve the language rather than the other way around. "Focus & Create" needed a quiet corner with good light — that single phrase determined the spatial logic of the experience section. When copy comes first, design solves a real problem. When design comes first and copy is filled in later, language becomes decoration.

02
Academic writing trains the discipline of evidence

A research paper cannot assert without evidence. Every claim must be supported, every source accurately represented, every argument traceable to something real. That discipline — which feels like a constraint in academic writing — is exactly the discipline required in design. A design decision without a rationale is an assertion. A design decision supported by research, testing, or observed behaviour is an argument. Academic writing teaches the difference.

03
Requirements writing is the moment research becomes design

The gap between "users are frustrated with notification management" and "users must be able to configure which notifications appear during specific activity types" is a writing problem. Requirements writing is the discipline of converting observed need into actionable specification — precise enough to be tested, clear enough to be understood by anyone who reads it, and always written from the user's perspective rather than the system's capabilities.

04
Descriptive writing is accessible writing

The skill of describing what is visually obvious to someone who cannot see it — practised through video transcription — is the same skill required to write alt text, ARIA labels, and screen reader–friendly navigation. Accessibility is not a checklist appended at the end of a project. It is a writing practice applied throughout. Every interface element that has language has an opportunity to either include or exclude someone who cannot rely on visual context alone.

05
Reflective writing turns single experiences into transferable practice

The interview bias identified in a peer conversation about academic deadlines changed how every subsequent research session was conducted. The AI tools reflection changed how assistance was used throughout the hypertext paper. Reflection is not introspection for its own sake — it is the mechanism by which a single mistake becomes a lasting improvement. Writing that examines process is the most practical writing there is.

Writing is the discipline that holds every other discipline together. Research needs writing to become insight. Design needs writing to communicate intention. Accessibility needs writing to reach the people it's meant to include. A designer who cannot write clearly cannot design clearly — because design is, at its foundation, an act of communication. Every word in this portfolio is evidence of that.
